summary:
HbA1c is not an ideal biomarker for well-controlled diabetes because it provides only a lagged average of blood glucose levels and does not account for day-to-day fluctuations. Modern guidelines recognize this limitation and advocate for personalized goals alongside tools like continuous glucose monitors (CGMs), which offer more accurate reflections of real-world glycemic control.  

\boxed{B} The correct answer is **No**, indicating HbA1c is not ideally suited for assessing true diabetic control.

The summary focuses solely on clinically significant aspects related to whether HbA1c serves as an appropriate indicator of well-controlled diabetes—specifically addressing accuracy, representativeness over time, and alignment with contemporary standards—and avoids any extraneous details such as procedural logistics or historical background unrelated to efficacy in reflecting disease status.
